annotations.label: Symmetry of a structured object

anchor.snippet: a symmetry is a mapping of the object onto itself which preserves the structure

annotations.note: Mathlib has no single generic 'symmetry' notion; the closest is CategoryTheory.Aut (self-isomorphisms of an object), with structure-specific versions like MulAut, Equiv.Perm, Isometry.

annotations.label: Even function

anchor.snippet: then f is even if the following equation holds for all x and -x in the domain of f

annotations.note: Function.Even f is defined as ∀ a, f (-a) = f a, exactly matching the definition.

annotations.label: Examples of even functions

anchor.snippet: Examples of even functions include

annotations.note: Evenness facts for example functions exist (e.g. Real.cos_neg, abs_neg) but are not packaged as Function.Even instances.

annotations.label: Odd function

anchor.snippet: then f is odd if the following equation holds for all x and -x in the domain of f

annotations.note: Function.Odd f is defined as ∀ a, f (-a) = -(f a), exactly matching the definition.

annotations.label: Examples of odd functions

anchor.snippet: Examples of odd functions are

annotations.note: Oddness facts for example functions exist (e.g. Real.sin_neg) but are not packaged as Function.Odd instances.

annotations.label: Integral of an odd function over symmetric interval

anchor.snippet: The integral of an odd function from − A to + A is zero

annotations.note: The building block integral_comp_neg (∫ f(-x) over a..b = ∫ f over -b..-a) exists, but there is no named lemma stating ∫_{-A}^{A} of an odd function is zero.

annotations.label: Integral of an even function over symmetric interval

anchor.snippet: The integral of an even function from − A to + A is twice the integral from 0 to + A

annotations.note: Infrastructure (integral_comp_neg) exists but no named lemma states ∫_{-A}^{A} of an even function equals twice ∫_0^A.

annotations.label: Maclaurin series of an even function

anchor.snippet: The Maclaurin series of an even function includes only even powers.

annotations.note: No result relating parity of a function to the parity of its Taylor/Maclaurin coefficients was found.

annotations.label: Maclaurin series of an odd function

anchor.snippet: The Maclaurin series of an odd function includes only odd powers.

annotations.note: No result relating oddness of a function to its Maclaurin series containing only odd powers was found.

annotations.label: Fourier series of a periodic even function

anchor.snippet: The Fourier series of a periodic even function includes only cosine terms.

annotations.note: No theorem relating evenness of a periodic function to vanishing of sine Fourier coefficients was found.

annotations.label: Fourier series of a periodic odd function

anchor.snippet: The Fourier series of a periodic odd function includes only sine terms.

annotations.note: No theorem relating oddness of a periodic function to vanishing of cosine Fourier coefficients was found.

annotations.label: Symmetric matrix

anchor.snippet: a symmetric matrix is a square matrix that is equal to its transpose

annotations.note: Matrix.IsSymm A is defined as Aᵀ = A on Matrix n n α, exactly a square matrix equal to its transpose.

annotations.label: Only square matrices can be symmetric

anchor.snippet: only square matrices can be symmetric

annotations.note: Squareness is enforced structurally by the type of Matrix.IsSymm (defined only for Matrix n n α), not stated as a theorem.

annotations.label: Entries symmetric about the main diagonal

anchor.snippet: The entries of a symmetric matrix are symmetric with respect to the main diagonal

annotations.note: Matrix.IsSymm.apply gives A j i = A i j, i.e. entries are symmetric across the main diagonal.

annotations.label: A symmetric 3×3 matrix

anchor.snippet: the following 3×3 matrix is symmetric

annotations.note: A concrete worked example of a particular symmetric 3×3 matrix is not present as a named declaration.

annotations.label: Diagonal matrices are symmetric

anchor.snippet: Every square diagonal matrix is symmetric, since all off-diagonal entries are zero

annotations.note: Matrix.isSymm_diagonal proves (diagonal v).IsSymm for any vector v.

annotations.label: Real symmetric matrix as self-adjoint operator / Hermitian matrix

anchor.snippet: a real symmetric matrix represents a self-adjoint operator over a real inner product space

annotations.note: Matrix.isHermitian_iff_isSymmetric states IsHermitian A ↔ A.toEuclideanLin.IsSymmetric (self-adjoint operator); for real matrices IsHermitian coincides with IsSymm.

annotations.label: Symmetric group

anchor.snippet: The symmetric group S n (on a finite set of n symbols) is the group whose elements are all the permutations of the n symbols

annotations.note: Equiv.Perm α is the group of permutations of α (with group instance); Sₙ is the special case Equiv.Perm (Fin n).

annotations.label: Order of the symmetric group

anchor.snippet: it follows that the order (i.e., the number of elements) of the symmetric group S n is n !

annotations.note: Fintype.card_perm proves Fintype.card (Perm α) = (Fintype.card α)!.

annotations.label: Symmetric polynomial

anchor.snippet: A symmetric polynomial is a polynomial P ( X 1 , X 2 , ..., X n ) in n variables

annotations.note: MvPolynomial.IsSymmetric φ is defined as invariance under all permutations of variables via rename, matching the definition.

annotations.label: Fundamental theorem of symmetric polynomials

anchor.snippet: A theorem states that any symmetric polynomial can be expressed in terms of elementary symmetric polynomials

annotations.note: esymmAlgHom_surjective (and esymmAlgEquiv) show every symmetric polynomial lies in the image of the elementary symmetric polynomials.

annotations.label: Symmetric polynomials in two variables

anchor.snippet: In two variables X 1 and X 2 , one has symmetric polynomials such as

annotations.note: The example polynomials (e.g. X₁+X₂, X₁X₂) are elementary symmetric polynomials, proven symmetric by esymm_isSymmetric, but not stated as standalone examples.

annotations.label: Symmetric polynomial in three variables

anchor.snippet: and in three variables X 1 , X 2 and X 3 , one has as a symmetric polynomial

annotations.note: Such examples are power-sum / elementary symmetric polynomials (psum, esymm) proven symmetric, but the specific three-variable instance is not a named declaration.

annotations.label: Symmetric tensor

anchor.snippet: a symmetric tensor is tensor that is invariant under a permutation of its vector arguments

annotations.note: SymmetricPower (Sym[R] ι M) is the tensor power quotiented by the permutation relation, formalizing tensors invariant under permutation of arguments.

annotations.label: Symmetric tensors and homogeneous polynomials

anchor.snippet: The space of symmetric tensors of rank r on a finite-dimensional vector space is naturally isomorphic to the dual

annotations.note: SymmetricPower is defined, but the file explicitly lists relating it to homogeneous polynomials as a TODO, so the isomorphism is not yet proven.

annotations.label: Central idea of Galois theory

anchor.snippet: The central idea of Galois theory is to consider those permutations (or rearrangements) of the roots

annotations.note: Polynomial.Gal acts on the roots via galActionHom into Equiv.Perm (rootSet), with galActionHom_injective showing the faithful permutation action of the Galois group on roots.

annotations.label: Automorphism

anchor.snippet: an automorphism is an isomorphism from a mathematical object to itself

annotations.note: CategoryTheory.Aut X := X ≅ X is the self-isomorphisms of an object, exactly an automorphism.

annotations.label: Automorphism group

anchor.snippet: The set of all automorphisms of an object forms a group , called the automorphism group

annotations.note: CategoryTheory.Aut X carries a Group instance, formalizing that automorphisms of an object form a group.

annotations.label: Set automorphisms form the symmetric group

anchor.snippet: In set theory , an arbitrary permutation of the elements of a set X is an automorphism

annotations.note: Equiv.Perm X is the group of bijections (automorphisms) of the set X, i.e. its symmetric group.

annotations.label: Automorphisms of the integers

anchor.snippet: the set of integers , Z , considered as a group under addition, has a unique nontrivial automorphism: negation

annotations.note: No declaration was found establishing that negation is the unique nontrivial additive automorphism of ℤ.

annotations.label: Group automorphism and inner automorphisms

anchor.snippet: A group automorphism is a group isomorphism from a group to itself

annotations.note: MulAut G is the group of self-isomorphisms of a group, and MulAut.conj gives the inner automorphisms g ↦ g·_·g⁻¹.

annotations.label: Vector space automorphisms and GL(V)

anchor.snippet: an endomorphism of a vector space V is a linear operator V → V

annotations.note: LinearMap.GeneralLinearGroup R M := (M →ₗ[R] M)ˣ is the group of invertible linear operators, i.e. GL(V) (endomorphisms are Module.End).

annotations.label: Field automorphism

anchor.snippet: A field automorphism is a bijective ring homomorphism from a field to itself

annotations.note: RingAut R := RingEquiv R R is the group of ring automorphisms; a field automorphism is this applied to a field.

annotations.label: Bosons symmetric, fermions antisymmetric

anchor.section: Symmetry in quantum mechanics: bosons and fermions
anchor.snippet: bosons have representatives that are symmetric under permutation operators, and fermions have antisymmetric representatives

annotations.note: This quantum-mechanical characterization of bosons/fermions via wavefunction symmetry is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Pauli exclusion equivalent to antisymmetric wavefunction

anchor.section: Symmetry in quantum mechanics: bosons and fermions
anchor.snippet: the Pauli exclusion principle with a single-valued many-particle wavefunction is equivalent to requiring the wavefunction to be antisymmetric

annotations.note: The Pauli exclusion principle and its equivalence to wavefunction antisymmetry are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Vanishing diagonal implies antisymmetry

anchor.section: Symmetry in quantum mechanics: bosons and fermions
anchor.snippet: Conversely, if the diagonal quantities A ( x , x ) are zero in every basis

annotations.note: For a bilinear form, IsAlt (B x x = 0 for all x) implies -B x y = B y x via IsAlt.neg_eq, i.e. vanishing diagonal forces antisymmetry.

annotations.label: Symmetric relation

anchor.snippet: We call a relation symmetric if every time the relation stands from A to B, it stands too from B to A

annotations.note: Symmetric r is defined as ∀ ⦃x y⦄, r x y → r y x, exactly matching the definition.

annotations.label: Isometry

anchor.snippet: An isometry is a distance -preserving map between metric spaces

annotations.note: Isometry f is defined as preserving the (extended) distance edist (f x) (f y) = edist x y.

annotations.label: Congruent figures via isometry

anchor.snippet: two geometric figures are congruent if they are related by an isometry

annotations.note: Congruent v₁ v₂ is defined via equal pairwise distances of indexed point sets, the metric-space formulation of being related by an isometry.

annotations.label: Symmetry of a differential equation

anchor.snippet: A symmetry of a differential equation is a transformation that leaves the differential equation invariant

annotations.note: Symmetries of differential equations are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Line (Lie) symmetry of a system of differential equations

anchor.snippet: A Line symmetry of a system of differential equations is a continuous symmetry of the system of differential equations

annotations.note: Lie (continuous) point symmetries of differential equations are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Lie symmetries yield first integrals

anchor.snippet: knowledge of an appropriate set of Lie symmetries allows one to explicitly calculate a set of first integrals

annotations.note: The relationship between Lie symmetries and first integrals of differential equations is not formalized.

annotations.label: Permutation symmetry gives discrete uniform distribution

anchor.snippet: symmetry with respect to permutations (relabelings) implies a discrete uniform distribution

annotations.note: The discrete uniform distribution (PMF.uniformOfFintype) is defined, but no theorem characterizes it as the unique permutation-invariant distribution.

annotations.label: Interval symmetry gives continuous uniform distribution

anchor.snippet: symmetry with respect to interchanging sub-intervals of equal length corresponds to a continuous uniform distribution

annotations.note: The continuous uniform distribution (IsUniform) is defined, but its characterization via interval-interchange symmetry is not formalized.

annotations.label: Reflection in a point preserving a distribution

anchor.snippet: There is one type of isometry in one dimension that may leave the probability distribution unchanged, that is reflection in a point

annotations.note: The notion of a probability distribution invariant under reflection (a symmetric distribution) is not formalized as such.

annotations.label: Reciprocal-invariance symmetry for positive outcomes

anchor.snippet: the outcome and its reciprocal have the same distribution

annotations.note: Distributions invariant under taking reciprocals are not formalized in Mathlib.
